notes: literal: ill or unwell vs slang: excellent/amazing
literal: a drug/substance vs slang: very good or impressive
literal: poor or undesirable vs slang: very good/cool (ironic reversal)
examples: His new song is very good.

notes: literal: burning/flames vs slang: outstanding or excellent
literal: past tense of light or illuminated vs slang: lively/exciting (or intensely fun)
examples: The room was lit by candles.
The room was lit by candles.
The party was lively and exciting.

notes: literal: low temperature vs slang: emotionally distant or unemotional
literal: recently made or new vs slang: impudent/rude or stylish depending on context
literal: rhythm or to strike vs slang: very tired/exhausted
literal: tasting of salt vs slang: bitter or resentful
literal: spirit/apparition vs slang: to deliberately ignore someone
examples: They say the house is haunted by a ghost.
They say the house is haunted by a ghost.
He ghosted her after the date.
He stopped responding to her after the date.

notes: literal: to strike vs slang: a big success or popular item
examples: The new single is a hit.
The new single is very popular.

notes: literal: to move smoothly, go down a surface vs slang: to approach someone discreetly (often by message)
examples: Children love to slide at the playground.
Children love to slide at the playground.
He tried to slide into her DMs.
He tried to send her a private direct message.

notes: literal: to bend or contract a muscle vs slang: to show off or boast
examples: He's always flexing his new car.
He is always showing off his new car.

notes: literal: to cook with dry heat vs slang: to mock or tease someone humorously
examples: Please roast the chicken for an hour.
Please roast the chicken for an hour.
They roasted him at the party.
They made fun of him at the party.

notes: literal: airborne particles from combustion vs slang: to defeat decisively
examples: She smoked her opponent in the match.
She defeated her opponent decisively in the match.

notes: literal: a hat vs slang: a lie or false statement (as in 'that's cap')
examples: He said he got a promotion, but that's cap.
He said he got a promotion, but that is a lie.

notes: literal: an area out of direct sun/shadow vs slang: a subtle insulting remark or disrespect
examples: He threw shade at her comment.
He made a subtle insulting remark about her comment.

notes: literal: stage objects used in performances vs slang: respect or credit given to someone
examples: Give her props for finishing early.
Give her credit for finishing early.

notes: literal: to compress or squish vs slang: a strong romantic attraction
examples: She has a crush on her classmate.
She is romantically attracted to her classmate.

notes: literal: to send goods by transport vs slang: to support a romantic pairing between people/characters
examples: They will ship the package tomorrow.
They will ship the package tomorrow.
Fans support the idea that those two characters are a romantic couple.

notes: literal: understated or not prominent vs slang: somewhat/quietly (adverbial soft emphasis)
examples: The event had a low-key presentation.
The event had a low-key presentation.
I'm low-key excited about the trip.
I am somewhat excited about the trip.

notes: literal: a playing card or an unreturnable serve vs slang: to do extremely well or succeed thoroughly
examples: He served an ace in the game.
He served an ace in the game.
She performed extremely well in the interview.

notes: literal: injury from heat or flame vs slang: an insulting remark that stings
examples: He got a burn on his arm.
He got a burn on his arm.
That comment was an insulting remark that hurt.

notes: literal: to suspend vs slang: to spend time socially ('hang out')
examples: Please hang the picture on the wall.
Please hang the picture on the wall.
Do you want to hang this weekend?
Do you want to spend time together this weekend?

notes: literal: toward a lower place vs slang: willing or agreeable ('I'm down')
examples: The cat jumped down from the table.
The cat jumped down from the table.

notes: literal: noun meaning police officer vs slang verb meaning to obtain or buy something
examples: The cop arrived at the scene.
The cop arrived at the scene.
I need to cop tickets for the show.
I need to buy tickets for the show.

notes: literal: firing a projectile or medical injection vs slang: an attempt or try
examples: She took a shot with the rifle.
She took a shot with the rifle.
I'll take a shot at solving it.
I will try to solve it.

notes: literal: to record points vs slang: to obtain or secure something (non-sexual)
examples: He scored three goals in the match.
He scored three goals in the match.
She scored tickets to the concert.
She obtained tickets to the concert.

notes: literal: a crash or heavy damage vs slang: to overwhelm, ruin, or emotionally drain someone
examples: The car wreck blocked the road.
The car wreck blocked the road.

notes: literal: to signify or the mathematical average vs slang: excellent/skilled (or alternatively cruel) depending on context; here: very good/skilled
literal temperature vs slang 'popular/attractive/trending'
examples: That movie is very popular right now.

notes: literal garbage vs slang 'very bad/worthless'
literal explosive/device vs slang 'fail badly' (or sometimes 'be a big success')
examples: They found a bomb in the field.
They found a bomb in the field.
The show failed badly last night.

notes: literal beverage vs slang 'gossip/information'
examples: She poured tea into the cup.
She poured tea into the cup.

notes: literal electrical connector vs slang 'a contact or source for goods/services'
examples: Unplug the charger from the wall.
Unplug the charger from the wall.
I need a plug for concert tickets.
I need a contact who can get me concert tickets.

notes: literal stone vs slang 'to wear/perform/appear well; to be excellent'
examples: She looks great in that dress.

notes: literal 'inflated' or pumped with fluid vs slang 'excited/energized'
examples: They pumped air into the tire.
They pumped air into the tire.
I am excited for the game.

notes: literal choreographed dangerous action vs slang 'show off'
examples: The actor did a dangerous stunt.
The actor did a dangerous stunt.
He likes to stunt on his friends.
He likes to show off in front of his friends.

notes: literal sheet or document vs slang 'money'
examples: Write your name on the paper.
Write your name on the paper.
He is trying to make money.

notes: literal rebound/elastic movement vs slang 'leave/depart'
examples: The ball will bounce on the floor.
The ball will bounce on the floor.

notes: literal past of 'shake' vs slang 'shocked/upset'
examples: I was shook by the news.
I was shocked by the news.

notes: literal beverage vs slang 'influence, power, or credibility'
examples: She has a lot of juice in the company.
She has a lot of influence in the company.

notes: literal 'to stir up a fire' vs slang 'very excited'
examples: He stoked the fire with more wood.
He stoked the fire with more wood.
I am very excited about the trip.

notes: literal 'murderer' vs slang 'extremely good/powerful'
literal 'reduce to small particles' vs slang 'work very hard / persistent effort'
examples: I have to grind all night to finish the project.
I have to work very hard all night to finish the project.

notes: literal 'tear' vs slang 'criticize harshly / make fun of / copy from media'
examples: They ripped the coach in the review.
They harshly criticized the coach in the review.

notes: literal 'broken/damaged' vs slang 'caught/arrested/exposed'
examples: The thief got busted last night.
The thief was arrested last night.

notes: literal vehicle or journey vs slang 'criticize someone or give them a hard time'
examples: We went for a ride in the car.
We went for a ride in the car.
She will ride him for missing practice.
She will criticize him for missing practice.

notes: literal serving utensil vs slang 'exclusive news or inside information'
examples: Use a scoop to serve ice cream.
Use a scoop to serve ice cream.
The reporter got the scoop on the story.
The reporter got the exclusive news about the story.

notes: literal pile vs slang 'a large amount of money'
examples: Stack the plates on the table.
Stack the plates on the table.
He has a stack of cash.
He has a large amount of cash.

notes: literal herb or candy flavor vs slang 'in perfect/new condition'
examples: This candy has a mint flavor.
This candy has a mint flavor.
His car is in perfect condition.

notes: literal strike with the hand vs slang 'be excellent/enjoyable (esp. music)'
literal car part used in gear changes vs slang 'crucial or performing under pressure'
examples: Press the clutch pedal to change gears.
Press the clutch pedal to change gears.
Her shot was clutch at the end.
Her shot was critical and came at an important moment.

notes: literal 'submerge briefly' or a sauce vs slang 'leave/exit'
examples: Dip the chip into the salsa.
Dip the chip into the salsa.

notes: literal browned bread vs slang 'doomed/finished'
examples: After that mistake, you are finished or in serious trouble.

notes: literal 'equipped with wires' vs slang 'highly alert, nervous, or caffeinated'
examples: The house is wired for electricity.
The house is wired for electricity.
He is very jittery and alert after the coffee.

notes: literal small stain or place vs slang 'lend money or notice/identify'
examples: I saw a spot on the shirt.
I saw a spot on the shirt.
Can you spot me five dollars?
Can you lend me five dollars?

notes: literal to turn over or form into a cylinder vs slang 'go/leave or move to an event'
examples: Roll the dough into a circle.
Roll the dough into a circle.
We should roll to the party.
We should go to the party.

notes: literal scatter liquid vs slang 'spend money freely to impress'
examples: Don't splash water on the floor.
Don't splash water on the floor.
They splashed out on a new car.
They spent a lot of money on a new car.

notes: literal dairy product vs slang 'defeat overwhelmingly'
examples: They got creamed in the game.
They were defeated by a large margin in the game.

notes: literal: stumble or fall vs slang: worry or overreact
examples: Be careful or you might trip on the step.
Be careful or you might trip on the step.
Don't trip about the homework, it's fine.
Don't worry about the homework, it's fine.

notes: literal: article of clothing vs slang: business executive or corporate person
examples: He packed his suit for the trip.
He packed his suit for the trip.
The suits in the meeting made the final call.
The executives in the meeting made the final call.

notes: literal: collide or have an accident vs slang: sleep suddenly or stay over informally
examples: The car crashed at the intersection.
The car crashed at the intersection.
Can I crash on your couch tonight?
Can I sleep on your couch tonight?

notes: literal: let something fall vs slang: release publicly (e.g., music)
examples: They dropped a new single last week.
They released a new single last week.

notes: literal: apply physical pressure vs slang: media coverage or public attention
examples: Press the red button to start the machine.
Press the red button to start the machine.
She got a lot of press after the event.
She received a lot of media coverage after the event.

notes: literal: small drop of liquid vs slang: fashionable style or clothing
examples: There is a drip coming from the faucet.
There is a drip coming from the faucet.
He really has drip with those shoes.
He really has a lot of style with those shoes.

notes: literal: deliver (e.g., tennis) vs slang: perform impressively or present oneself well
examples: She will serve the ball at the next game.
She will serve the ball at the next game.
She served on the runway last night.
She performed very well on the runway last night.

notes: literal: high temperature vs slang: strong performance or intense pressure
examples: Turn on the heat, it's cold in here.
Turn on the heat, it's cold in here.
The team really brought the heat in the second half.
The team performed very intensely in the second half.

notes: literal: cover or enclose vs slang: finish or conclude (often production)
examples: That's a wrap on the shoot.
That is the end of the shoot.

notes: literal: fire a gun or take a photograph vs slang: send a quick message or ask to contact
examples: Don't shoot the gun in the house.
Don't shoot the gun in the house.
Shoot me a message when you arrive.
Send me a message when you arrive.

notes: literal: break suddenly vs slang: suddenly lose emotional control or react strongly
examples: She snapped the twig in half.
She snapped the twig in half.
He snapped after the long meeting.
He lost control emotionally after the long meeting.

notes: literal: turn around vs slang: present information in a favorable or biased way
examples: Spin the wheel to see where the arrow lands.
Spin the wheel to see where the arrow lands.
The party tried to spin the story to their advantage.
The party tried to present the story in a way that benefited them.

notes: literal: condiment or liquid food topping vs slang: charisma, style, or social clout
examples: Add some sauce to the pasta.
Add some sauce to the pasta.
He has the sauce when it comes to fashion.
He has a lot of style when it comes to fashion.

notes: literal: sleeve edge or handcuffing vs slang: start a serious romantic relationship (cuffing)
examples: Fold the cuff of your shirt before washing.
Fold the cuff of your shirt before washing.
He started a committed relationship with her last winter.

notes: literal: implement for driving animals vs slang: one's car (slang for vehicle)
examples: The cowboy used a whip to guide the horse.
The cowboy used a whip to guide the horse.
He washed his new whip yesterday.
He washed his new car yesterday.

notes: literal: produce milk or extract milk vs slang: exploit or take advantage of something for benefit
examples: The farmer milks the cows every morning.
The farmer milks the cows every morning.
She milked the situation for attention.
She exploited the situation for attention.

notes: literal: engage in a game or performance vs slang: manipulate someone or act romantically/deceptively
examples: The children play in the park.
The children play in the park.
Don't play him if you want to be honest.
Don't deceive him if you want to be honest.

notes: literal: drop off or discard vs slang: end a romantic relationship
examples: Please dump the trash in the bin.
Please dump the trash in the bin.
She dumped him after the argument.
She broke up with him after the argument.

notes: literal: fasten with a nail or finger nail vs slang: do something perfectly or succeed
examples: Use a nail to hang the picture.
Use a nail to hang the picture.
She nailed the interview this morning.
She did very well in the interview this morning.

notes: literal: possess or have ownership vs slang: defeat decisively
examples: I own that car free and clear.
I own that car free and clear.
He owned his opponent in the debate.
He defeated his opponent in the debate.

notes: literal: lose color or brightness vs slang: ignore someone or reduce attention to them
examples: The color will fade in the sun.
The color will fade in the sun.
After the date he just faded her.
After the date he stopped responding to her.

notes: literal: the outer side or facade vs slang: pretend or put on a false appearance
examples: The front of the building needs repainting.
The front of the building needs repainting.
Don't front about your knowledge of the topic.
Don't pretend you know more about the topic than you do.

notes: literal: floor of a ship or outdoor platform vs slang: knock someone to the ground or defeat physically
examples: We sat on the deck to watch the sunset.
We sat on the deck to watch the sunset.
He decked the attacker in one punch.
He knocked the attacker to the ground with one punch.

notes: literal: move back and forth vs slang: visit briefly or stop by
examples: The child likes to swing at the playground.
The child likes to swing at the playground.
Can you swing by my place later?
Can you stop by my place later?

notes: literal: remove outer skin vs slang: leave or depart quickly
examples: Peel the orange before you eat it.
Peel the orange before you eat it.
I gotta peel, see you tomorrow.
I have to leave; see you tomorrow.

notes: literal: lightly touch or draw liquid from a container vs slang: select or recruit someone for a role
examples: They tapped her to lead the project.
They selected her to lead the project.

notes: literal: ruin or go bad (food) vs slang: indulge someone excessively
examples: If you leave milk out it will spoil.
If you leave milk out it will spoil.
Her grandparents always spoil her with gifts.
Her grandparents always indulge her with gifts.

notes: literal: manager or employer vs slang: excellent or impressive (as an adjective) or to control authoritatively
examples: My boss approved my vacation request.
My boss approved my vacation request.

notes: literal: make a small popping sound or burst vs slang: become suddenly popular or energetic
examples: The balloon will pop if you overinflate it.
The balloon will pop if you overinflate it.
That song became very popular last summer.

notes: literal: step heavily on something vs slang: defeat thoroughly or dominate
examples: Don't stomp on the flowers in the garden.
Don't stomp on the flowers in the garden.
They stomped the other team in the finals.
They defeated the other team decisively in the finals.

notes: literal: past of 'jack' (lift) vs slang: very muscular
examples: He jacked the car to change the tire.
He jacked the car to change the tire.
He is jacked after months at the gym.
He is very muscular after months at the gym.

notes: literal: edible nuts or nutty food vs slang: crazy/extremely excited
examples: She bought a bag of nuts.
She bought a bag of nuts.
He went nuts during the game.
He became very excited during the game.

notes: literal: kill or defeat vs slang: perform extremely well or impress
examples: The knight will slay the dragon.
The knight will slay the dragon.
She performs exceptionally well at every performance.

notes: literal: past tense of wake (awake) vs slang: socially/politically aware
examples: She is aware of the social issue.

notes: literal: vibration or physical sensation vs slang: atmosphere or general feeling
examples: I felt the vibe from the old speaker.
I felt the vibe from the old speaker.
I like the vibe of this cafe.
I like the atmosphere of this cafe.

notes: literal: fierce or untamed vs slang: brutally harsh or impressively ruthless
examples: The wolf is savage when cornered.
The wolf is savage when cornered.
That was a brutally harsh insult.

notes: literal: cause death vs slang: do extremely well or defeat thoroughly
examples: They will kill the weeds in the garden.
They will kill the weeds in the garden.
She did extremely well on the exam.

notes: literal: a small hit or raised area vs slang: play music loudly or promote something
examples: I hit a bump on the road.
I hit a bump on the road.
They bumped the new song all night.
They played the new song loudly all night.

notes: literal: free of dirt vs slang: precise, polished, or skillful
examples: That routine is very precise and polished.

notes: literal: have difficulty breathing vs slang: fail under pressure
examples: He choked on a fish bone.
He choked on a fish bone.
He choked in the final minutes.
He failed under pressure in the final minutes.

notes: literal: a contest or sport vs slang: skill with people (social/romantic skill)
examples: He has game with new people.
He is socially skilled with new people.

notes: literal: hit repeatedly with a hammer vs slang: very drunk
examples: He hammered the nail into the wood.
He hammered the nail into the wood.
After the party he was hammered.
After the party he was very drunk.

notes: literal: divide or cut apart vs slang: leave quickly
examples: We split the log for firewood.
We split the log for firewood.

notes: literal: remove water from a boat or use a bail (bucket) vs slang: abandon plans or back out
examples: They bailed water out of the boat.
They bailed water out of the boat.
He canceled at the last minute and did not attend the meeting.

notes: literal: the color blue vs slang: feeling sad or down
literal: the color green vs slang: inexperienced or new at something
examples: He is inexperienced at that job.

notes: literal: publicity or exaggerated promotion vs slang: excited attention or buzz
examples: The ad used a lot of hype.
The ad used a lot of hype.
People are hyping the new show.
People are promoting the new show heavily.

notes: literal: a container vs slang: secure or obtain (often informally claim/achieve)
examples: She put the bread in a bag.
She put the bread in a bag.

notes: literal: a quick light hit vs slang: a very good or catchy song
examples: She gave the toy a little bop.
She gave the toy a little bop.
This song is very good and catchy.

notes: literal: ground or surface vs slang: overwhelm or greatly surprise
examples: She dropped the plate on the floor.
She dropped the plate on the floor.

notes: literal: uncooked or unprocessed vs slang: unfiltered or emotionally intense
examples: That interview felt unfiltered and emotionally intense.

notes: literal: the metal gold vs slang: extremely valuable or excellent
examples: The ring is made of gold.
The ring is made of gold.

notes: literal: to dig a hole vs slang: to like or appreciate
examples: They dig a hole for the tree.
They dig a hole for the tree.

notes: literal: an explosion or strong gust vs slang: a very enjoyable time
examples: We had a blast at the party.
We had a great time at the party.

notes: literal: something used to catch fish vs slang: to deliberately provoke or lure someone
examples: They used bait to catch fish.
They used bait to catch fish.
He tried to provoke her into replying.

notes: literal: an instrument for viewing or the range of something vs slang: to look around or check out (scope out)
examples: He scoped the room before entering.
He looked around the room before entering.

notes: literal: a baby's bed vs slang: someone's home or living place
examples: The baby slept in the crib.
The baby slept in the crib.
They went to his crib after the show.
They went to his house after the show.

notes: literal: a quick drawing vs slang: suspicious or untrustworthy (informal 'sketchy')
examples: She made a quick sketch of the scene.
She made a quick sketch of the scene.
That plan seems suspicious or untrustworthy.

notes: literal: an area or region vs slang: a focused mental state ('in the zone')
examples: He is in the zone during games.
He is highly focused during games.

notes: literal: an object that covers vs slang: take someone else's responsibility (e.g., a work shift)
examples: Please put a cover on the pot.
Please put a cover on the pot.
Can you cover my shift tomorrow?
Can you take my work shift tomorrow?
